facts.text: Buenaventura Lakes grew from rural farmland into a suburban enclave in the 1980s after targeted ads promised a palm‑tree paradise near Walt Disney World.
The area’s growth sparked cultural integration, with Spanish shaping local schools, businesses, and public life as Hispanics rose from 2% in 1980 to 45.5% by 2010.
Early land platting in the late 1960s turned the region into subdivided residential parcels, supported by roads and utilities from the Kissimmee Utility Authority’s predecessor.
Residents enjoy the Osceola Library System, which serves the community with books, programs, and digital resources.
Buenaventura Lakes is known as Little Puerto Rico or Little New York, reflecting its strong Puerto Rican and New Yorker heritage.
The CDP is part of the Orlando–Kissimmee–Sanford Metropolitan Statistical Area, linking it to Central Florida’s larger economy.
